section.title: The Request of James and John, and Bartimaeus
section.sectionRationale: The journey to Jerusalem climaxes. Jesus predicts His death a third time in stark detail, yet James and John ask for the best seats in glory, exposing how far the disciples still are from understanding. Jesus answers with the Gospel's key verse: the Son of Man came to serve and give His life as a ransom for many. Then blind Bartimaeus, who sees by faith what the others miss, calls Jesus Son of David, receives his sight, and follows Him on the road, a fitting close to this section on discipleship.
section.thesis: The Son of Man goes to Jerusalem to give His life as a ransom for many, and greatness in His kingdom is cross-shaped service, so that true sight belongs not to the ambitious disciples but to a blind beggar who calls on the Son of David and follows Him on the road to the cross.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the third Passion prediction; the request of James and John and the ransom saying; and the healing of blind Bartimaeus who follows Jesus on the way.

units.label: The third Passion prediction
units.note: we are going up to Jerusalem

verses.text: As they were going up the road to Jerusalem, Jesus was walking ahead of them. The disciples were amazed, but those who followed were afraid. Again Jesus took the Twelve aside and began to tell them what was going to happen to Him:
“Look, we are going up to Jerusalem, and the Son of Man will be delivered over to the chief priests and scribes. They will condemn Him to death and will deliver Him over to the Gentiles,
who will mock Him and spit on Him and flog Him and kill Him. And after three days He will rise again.”

units.label: The request of James and John
units.note: the Son of Man came to give His life a ransom

verses.text: Then James and John, the sons of Zebedee, came to Jesus and declared, “Teacher, we want You to do for us whatever we ask.”
“What do you want Me to do for you?” He inquired.
They answered, “Grant that one of us may sit at Your right hand and the other at Your left in Your glory.”
“You do not know what you are asking,” Jesus replied. “Can you drink the cup I will drink, or be baptized with the baptism I will undergo?”
“We can,” the brothers answered. “You will drink the cup that I drink,” Jesus said, “and you will be baptized with the baptism that I undergo.
But to sit at My right or left is not Mine to grant. These seats belong to those for whom they have been prepared.”
When the ten heard about this, they became indignant with James and John.
So Jesus called them together and said, “You know that those regarded as rulers of the Gentiles lord it over them, and their superiors exercise authority over them.
But it shall not be this way among you. Instead, whoever wants to become great among you must be your {{diakoneo|servant}},
and whoever wants to be first must be the slave of all.
For even the Son of Man did not come to be served, but to serve, and to give His life as a {{lytron|ransom}} for many.”

units.label: Blind Bartimaeus follows on the way
units.note: Son of David, have mercy on me

verses.text: Next, they came to Jericho. And as Jesus and His disciples were leaving Jericho with a large crowd, a blind beggar named Bartimaeus, the son of Timaeus, was sitting beside the road.
When he heard that it was Jesus of Nazareth, he began to cry out, “Jesus, {{huiosdavid|Son of David}}, have mercy on me!”
Many people admonished him to be silent, but he cried out all the louder, “Son of David, have mercy on me!”
Jesus stopped and said, “Call him.” So they called the blind man. “Take courage!” they said. “Get up! He is calling for you.”
Throwing off his cloak, Bartimaeus jumped up and came to Jesus.
“What do you want Me to do for you?” Jesus asked. “{{rabbouni|Rabboni}},” said the blind man, “let me see again.”
“Go,” said Jesus, “your faith has healed you.” And immediately he received his sight and followed Jesus along the road.

diakoneo.gloss: To serve, to wait on, to minister to. Against the world's model of lording it over others, Jesus makes service the measure of greatness in His kingdom, and points to Himself as the servant who serves to the uttermost, giving His life.

lytron.gloss: A ransom, the price paid to release a captive or slave. Jesus interprets His death as a ransom given in the place of many, a substitutionary payment that sets captives free. It is the clearest statement in Mark of why He came to die.

huiosdavid.gloss: Son of David, a title for the promised Messiah of David's royal line. Blind Bartimaeus sees by faith what the sighted crowd misses, hailing Jesus as the messianic King even as He goes up to Jerusalem to reign through the cross.

rabbouni.gloss: My master, my teacher, an intensified and reverent form of Rabbi. Bartimaeus addresses Jesus with heartfelt respect and trust, and his simple request, let me see again, is answered as his faith lays hold of the Son of David.

basic.context: Jesus leads the way up to Jerusalem, and the disciples are amazed and afraid. He tells them plainly, for the third time, that He will be handed over, mocked, flogged, killed, and after three days rise. Astonishingly, James and John respond by asking for the top seats in His glory. Jesus says they do not know what they are asking, and that greatness in His kingdom is not lording it over others but serving, for even He came not to be served but to serve and to give His life as a ransom for many. Then, as they leave Jericho, blind Bartimaeus cries out to Jesus as Son of David, refuses to be silenced, receives his sight, and follows Jesus down the road toward the cross.
basic.hermeneutics: This closes the journey section and its lessons on discipleship. The contrast is deliberate: the disciples who can see grasp for status, while the blind man sees Jesus truly and follows Him. Verse 45 is the theological centre of Mark, explaining the meaning of the death Jesus has repeatedly foretold: a ransom in the place of many. Bartimaeus is the model disciple.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus teaches that the great must diakoneo, serve, and that He came to give His life as a lytron, a ransom, for many. Bartimaeus hails Him as the huiosdavid, the Son of David, and calls Him rabbouni, my master, receiving his sight and following.
basic.typology: The cup Jesus will drink is the cup of God's judgment in the prophets, which He drains for His people. The ransom fulfils the suffering servant of Isaiah 53 who bears the sin of many. The Son of David is the promised messianic King.
basic.christ: Jesus is the servant-King who gives His life as a ransom, the Son of David going up to Jerusalem to reign through the cross, and the One whose true identity a blind beggar confesses when the sighted cannot.
basic.god: God's kingdom runs by the reverse of the world's power. God accomplishes salvation through the self-giving of His Son, a ransom that frees captives, and He opens the eyes of those who cry out in faith.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 foretells the servant who bears the sin of many. Psalm 75 speaks of the cup of God's wrath. Isaiah 11 and 2 Samuel 7 promise the Son of David.
basic.oneStory: The road to Jerusalem is the road to the cross, and here Jesus states why He walks it: to give His life as a ransom for many. The disciples still crave glory, but a blind man shows the way, calling on the Son of David and following Him. The whole story now stands at the gates of Jerusalem, where the servant-King will pay the ransom price.

context.literary: This passage completes the central journey section, framed by two healings of blind men, the man of Bethsaida in chapter 8 and Bartimaeus here, bracketing Jesus' teaching on the cross and discipleship. The third and most detailed Passion prediction is met, as before, by a failure to understand, here the request for glory, and answered by teaching on servanthood crowned by the ransom saying.
context.historical: The road up to Jerusalem climbed steeply from Jericho. Sitting at the right and left hand signified the highest honour in a royal court. A blind beggar by the roadside depended wholly on alms; throwing off his cloak, likely used to collect coins, dramatizes his abandoning everything to come to Jesus. Son of David was a charged messianic title in Jerusalem's shadow.
context.canonical: The ransom saying, Mark 10:45, distils Isaiah 53:10-12, where the servant pours out His life and bears the sin of many. The cup Jesus will drink is the cup of God's judgment, Psalm 75:8 and Isaiah 51:17, which He drains in His people's place. Son of David fulfils the covenant promise of 2 Samuel 7:12-16 and the shoot from Jesse in Isaiah 11:1.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reveal the purpose of Jesus' death as a ransom for many, to overturn the disciples' worldly ambition with the model of cross-shaped service, and, through Bartimaeus, to portray true discipleship as seeing Jesus by faith and following Him on the road to the cross.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The ransom saying describes the unrepeatable, saving work only Christ can do. The teaching on servant greatness is prescriptive for all disciples. Bartimaeus's healing describes a unique miracle, but his faith-filled cry and his following are held up as a pattern to imitate.

debates.question: What does it mean that Jesus gives His life as a ransom for many?

views.label: A substitutionary payment in the place of many
views.case: The Greek preposition and the ransom image convey Jesus dying in the stead of many, paying the price to free those held captive to sin and death, fulfilling the servant who bears the sin of many in Isaiah 53.

views.label: A liberating price that redefines power
views.case: Set against rulers who lord it over others, the ransom shows a King who reigns by self-giving; His death is the costly act that releases captives and establishes a kingdom of service rather than domination.

debates.bearing: Both readings hold that Jesus' death is purposeful and saving, a costly self-substitution that liberates many and models the servanthood His people are called to.

debates.question: Why does Mark frame this section with the healing of two blind men?

views.label: A parable of spiritual sight
views.case: The bracketing healings mirror the disciples' journey from blindness to sight; Bartimaeus, who sees Jesus as Son of David and follows Him, embodies the clear sight the disciples still lack even as they near Jerusalem.

views.label: A summons to follow on the way
views.case: Bartimaeus followed Jesus along the road, the very road to the cross; his healing becomes a call to true discipleship, seeing Christ rightly and following Him into suffering and glory.

debates.bearing: Both agree Bartimaeus is the model disciple: he sees who Jesus is, calls on Him in faith, and follows Him on the way, in pointed contrast to the status-seeking Twelve.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus overturns the world's ladder: the great must diakoneo, serve, and the first must be slave of all, for even the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve and to give His life as a lytron, a ransom, the price that buys captives free, in the place of many. Then a blind beggar, seeing by faith what the sighted crowd cannot, cries out to the huiosdavid, the Son of David, Israel's promised King, and addresses Jesus as rabbouni, my master, in trust and reverence. His sight restored, he follows Jesus down the road to Jerusalem, the very picture of the discipleship the ambitious Twelve have missed.

typology.fulfillment: The suffering servant who pours out His life and bears the sin of many, Isaiah 53:10-12 and 1 Timothy 2:6.
The cup of God's judgment which Christ drains in His people's place, Psalm 75:8 and Isaiah 51:17.
Jesus as the promised messianic King of David's line, 2 Samuel 7:12-16 and Isaiah 11:1-10.

study.christ: Here Jesus states most clearly why He has set His face to Jerusalem: the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give His life as a ransom for many. He is the servant-King who reigns by self-giving, draining the cup of judgment His people deserved. When James and John grasp for thrones, He points them instead to the cross-shaped path He walks first. And a blind beggar, crying out to the Son of David, confesses the messianic identity the crowds and even the Twelve keep missing, then follows Jesus on the road to Calvary. The King goes up to Jerusalem to pay the ransom price and to open blind eyes, both physical and spiritual.
study.god: God's kingdom is the great reversal of human power. Where the rulers of the nations lord it over others, God's King leads by serving, and God accomplishes the salvation of many through the self-giving death of His Son, a ransom that liberates captives no human effort could free. God honours the humble cry of a blind beggar over the ambition of insiders, opening his eyes and drawing him onto the road of discipleship. The passage reveals a God who saves at infinite cost to Himself and who exalts service over status.

crossReferences.note: The servant pours out His life and bears the sin of many, the prophecy behind Jesus' ransom saying.
The cup in the LORD's hand of foaming wine, the cup of judgment Jesus says He will drink.
God's covenant to establish David's offspring and throne forever, fulfilled in Jesus, the Son of David.
Christ Jesus, who gave Himself as a ransom for all, echoing Mark 10:45.

study.oneStory: The road to Jerusalem is the road to the cross, and Jesus now says plainly why He walks it: to give His life as a ransom for many. The world's way is to grasp for thrones, and even James and John are caught in it, but the King's way is to serve and to die. A blind beggar sees this King more truly than the sighted, hails Him as the Son of David, and follows Him on the way. The whole story now stands at the gates of the holy city, where the servant-King will drink the cup of judgment and pay the ransom that sets His people free.

quiz.q: What did Jesus foretell for the third time as they went up to Jerusalem?

options.text: That the Son of Man would be handed over, mocked, flogged, killed, and after three days rise again.
options.note: Mark 10:33-34 gives the detailed third Passion prediction.

options.text: That He would suffer and die in Jerusalem.
options.note: True but partial; this prediction adds the mocking, flogging, handing to the Gentiles, and rising again.

options.text: That He would enter Jerusalem and be crowned king by the crowds.
options.note: He would be rejected and killed, not crowned by the crowds, though He reigns through the cross.

options.text: That the disciples would be arrested in His place.
options.note: Jesus foretold His own suffering and death, not the disciples' arrest.

quiz.q: How did James and John respond to Jesus' prediction of His death?

options.text: They asked for the seats of highest honour at His right and left in His glory.
options.note: Mark 10:37; they grasped for status even as Jesus spoke of dying.

options.text: They asked to share in whatever Jesus was about to face.
options.note: They claimed they could drink His cup, but their actual request was for glory and honour, not suffering.

options.text: They sought positions of prominence in His coming kingdom.
options.note: Correct in substance, but specifically they asked for the seats at His right and left in glory.

options.text: They offered to die alongside Him.
options.note: They asked for honour, not to die with Him, though Jesus said they would share His cup.

quiz.q: How does Jesus define greatness, and what does He say about His own mission?

options.text: The great must be servants, for the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve and give His life as a ransom for many.
options.note: Mark 10:43-45, the theological heart of the Gospel.

options.text: The great must serve others rather than lord it over them.
options.note: True, but it omits the crucial reason: the Son of Man came to give His life as a ransom.

options.text: The great are those who gain the most authority for good ends.
options.note: Jesus contrasts kingdom greatness with the authority-seeking of worldly rulers.

options.text: Greatness comes from being honoured by others.
options.note: Jesus locates greatness in serving, not in receiving honour.

quiz.q: What does it mean that Jesus gives His life as a ransom for many?

options.text: His death is a substitutionary price paid in the place of many, setting captives free.
options.note: A ransom is a price to free captives; Jesus dies in the stead of many, fulfilling Isaiah 53.

options.text: His death is a costly act that liberates people and redefines power.
options.note: True, but it should include the substitutionary sense of a price paid in the place of many.

options.text: His death is mainly an example of self-sacrifice to inspire us.
options.note: It is an example, but far more: an actual ransom that frees captives, not merely inspiration.

options.text: His death pays a debt owed to the devil.
options.note: Scripture presents the ransom as satisfying God's justice and freeing captives, not a payment to the devil.

quiz.q: How does blind Bartimaeus model true discipleship?

options.text: He sees Jesus by faith as the Son of David, cries out for mercy, receives sight, and follows Him on the road.
options.note: Mark 10:47-52; he grasps Jesus' identity and follows Him toward the cross.

options.text: He persistently asked Jesus for help and was healed.
options.note: True, but it misses his confession of Jesus as Son of David and his following Him on the way.

options.text: He waited quietly until Jesus noticed him.
options.note: The opposite; he cried out all the louder despite being told to be silent.

options.text: He was healed but went home rather than following.
options.note: Mark says he followed Jesus along the road after receiving his sight.

questions: Jesus walked ahead toward the cross while the disciples were amazed and afraid. How does it move you that Jesus went first, resolutely, into suffering for your sake?
James and John sought status even as Jesus spoke of dying. Where do you catch yourself pursuing recognition instead of following the way of the cross?
Jesus said greatness is service. Whom is God calling you to serve this week in a way that costs you something?
Jesus gave His life as a ransom for you. How does dwelling on that price shape your gratitude and your worship?
Bartimaeus threw off his cloak, came to Jesus, and followed Him on the road. What might you need to throw off to follow Jesus more freely?

translationNotes.note: The cup and the baptism are images of the suffering and judgment Jesus will undergo; to drink the cup draws on the Old Testament cup of God's wrath, which He drains in His people's place.

A ransom for many renders lytron anti pollon; the word anti conveys in the place of, marking Jesus' death as a substitution, and many is inclusive, not restrictive, echoing the many of Isaiah 53:11-12.
Rabboni is an Aramaic term of deep respect, my master or my teacher, stronger than Rabbi; Bartimaeus addresses Jesus with trust and reverence.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Mark 10:32-52. Footnote markers and line breaks were dropped when assembling verse text. No em or en dashes appear in this passage. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
